elements.sent-2411-001: This article concerns the question of how the geometry of the horospheres of a closed negatively curved manifold of dimension greater than $2$ determines the geometry of the whole manifold.
elements.sent-2411-002: Relations between the extrinsic geometry of the horospheres and the geometry of $M$ have already been considered.
elements.sent-2411-003: For instance, \cite{FoL and \cite{BCG1 show that if all the horospheres have constant mean curvature, then the underlying manifold is locally symmetric (of negative curvature).}}
elements.sent-2411-004: Let us recall that the mean curvature of a hypersurface is related to the derivative of its volume element in the normal direction to the hypersurface, and hence the mean curvature is an extrinsic quantity.
elements.sent-2411-005: In contrast, we give in this work a characterization of the hyperbolic space in term of the an {\sl intrinsic geometric property of the horospheres.}
elements.sent-2411-006: Before stating our main theorem, let us recall a few important features of the manifolds under consideration and results that are related to our work in this paper.
elements.sent-2411-007: Let $(M, g)$ denote a $n$-dimensional, closed, Riemannian manifold endowed with a metric of negative sectional curvature and of dimension $n\geq 3$.
elements.sent-2411-008: It follows from the Cartan-Hadamard theorem that $\tilde M$, the universal cover of $M$ endowed with the metric $\tilde g$, is diffeomorphic to $\mathbf{R ^{n}$.}
elements.sent-2411-009: Given a point, $\tilde m_0\in \widetilde M$, and a unit tangent vector, $\tilde v\in T_{\tilde m_0}\widetilde M$, we let $c_{\tilde v}$ denote the unique geodesic ray determined by $c_{\tilde v (0)=\tilde m_0$ and $\dot c _{\tilde v (0) =\tilde v$.}}
elements.sent-2411-010: It is well known that the map, $\tilde v \in T_{x_0 \widetilde M \mapsto [c_{\tilde v ] \in \partial \tilde M$, defines a homeomorphism between the unit sphere in $T_{\tilde m_0 \widetilde M$ and $\partial \widetilde M$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-011: Given a point $\xi = [c_{\tilde v ] \in \partial \widetilde M$, the Busemann function $B_\xi(\cdot)$ is then defined for all $\xi \in \partial \widetilde M$ and for all $\tilde m \in \widetilde M$, by $B_{\xi (\tilde m)= \lim_{t\to \infty (d(\tilde m,c_{\tilde v (t)) - d(\tilde m_0, c_{\tilde v (t)))$.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-012: Since $M$ is a closed negatively curved manifold, it is known that for each $\xi \in \partial \widetilde M$ the Busemann function $B_\xi(\cdot)$ is $C^\infty$-smooth.
elements.sent-2411-013: Furthermore, for any $t\in\mathbf{R}$, the level set \begin{equation}H_{\xi}(t) =\left\{\tilde m\in {\widetilde M};\, B_\xi(\tilde m)= t\right\}\end{equation} is a smooth submanifold of $\widetilde M$ which is diffeomorphic to $\mathbf{R ^{n}$ and which is called a {\sl horosphere centred at $\xi$.}}
elements.sent-2411-014: It follows that horospheres inherit a complete Riemannian metric induced by the restriction of the metric of $\widetilde M$.
elements.sent-2411-015: For instance, if $(M,g)$ is a real hyperbolic manifold, every horosphere of $\widetilde M$ is flat and therefore isometric to the Euclidean space $\mathbf R^{n}$.
elements.sent-2411-016: We defined horospheres as special submanifolds in $\widetilde M$ and let us give now a dynamical perspective.
elements.sent-2411-017: Let $\tilde p : T^1\widetilde M \rightarrow \widetilde M$ and $p : T^1M \rightarrow M$ denote the natural projections.
elements.sent-2411-018: The geodesic flow on $T^1\widetilde M$ is known to be an Anosov flow, that is, the tangent bundle $TT^1 \widetilde M$ admits a decomposition as $TT^1\widetilde M = \mathbf R X \oplus \widetilde E^{ss \oplus \widetilde E^{su}$, where $X$ is the vector field generating the geodesic flow and $\widetilde E^{ss}$, $\widetilde E^{su}$ are the strong stable and strong unstable distributions, respectively.}
elements.sent-2411-019: These distributions are known to be integrable, invariant under the differential of the geodesic flow, and to give rise to two transverse foliations of $T^1\widetilde M$, $\widetilde W^{ss}$ and $\widetilde W^{su}$, the strong stable and strong unstable foliations, respectively, whose leaves are smooth submanifolds.
elements.sent-2411-020: A classical property of these foliations is that in general they are transversally H\" older with exponent less than one.
elements.sent-2411-021: A link between the two point of views on horospheres is the following.
elements.sent-2411-022: For $\tilde v\in T^1\widetilde M$, the strong unstable leaf $\widetilde W^{su (\tilde v)$ through $\tilde v$ is defined to be the set of unit vectors $\tilde w\in T^1\widetilde M$ which are normal to the horosphere $H_\xi (0)$ and pointing outward, that is, on the same side as $\tilde v$.}
elements.sent-2411-023: For the sake of simplicity this horosphere defined by $\tilde v$ will be noted in the sequel by $H(\tilde v)$.
elements.sent-2411-024: Note that the unstable foliation being in general only Hölder echoes the fact that the Busemann functions are only Hölder continuous in $\xi$; however, for any fixed $\xi$, we get a foliation of $\widetilde M$ by the whole family of horospheres centred at $\xi$ which is $C^\infty$-smooth as well as the Busemann function $B_\xi( .)$ as a function of $\tilde m\in \widetilde M$.
elements.sent-2411-025: More details are given in \cite{BCH , the Introduction and Section 2.}
elements.sent-2411-026: In sum, when $(M, g)$ is a closed negatively curved manifold the horospheres are smooth and carry the Riemannian metric induced by $\tilde g$.
elements.sent-2411-027: From the previous comments we know that the geometric quantities related to a given horosphere $H(\tilde v)$, such as its curvature and its second fundamental form, are continuous on $T^1\widetilde M$ and smooth along the flow lines.
elements.sent-2411-028: Our assumptions, stated in the Theorem below, are concerned with the scalar curvature of the horospheres.
elements.sent-2411-029: We let $\tilde s(\tilde v)$ denote the scalar curvature of $H(\tilde v)$ at the base point $\tilde p (\tilde v)$ of $\tilde v$.
elements.sent-2411-030: It is a continuous function defined on $T^1\widetilde M$.
elements.sent-2411-031: The natural projection $\pi: \widetilde M\rightarrow M$ maps $H(\tilde v)$ onto an immersed hypersurface in $M$ which is denoted, by abuse of language, by $H(v)$ where $m=\pi (\tilde m)$ and $v= d\pi (\tilde v)\in T^1_m(M)$.
elements.sent-2411-032: By invariance by the deck transformations, the scalar curvature $\tilde s (\cdot)$ descends to a smooth function defined on $T^1(M)$, and for $v\in T^1M$ we denote it by $s(v)$.
elements.sent-2411-033: We also let $\mu_L$ be the Liouville measure on $T^1M$.
elements.sent-2411-034: Our main theorem is the following.
elements.sent-2411-035: Let $(M^n, g)$ be a closed Riemannian manifold of dimension $n\geq 3$ and of negative curvature.
elements.sent-2411-037: Then $(M, g)$ has constant negative sectional curvature.
elements.sent-2411-038: Let us assume that one horosphere, $H(\tilde v_0)$ for some $\tilde v_0\in T^1_{\tilde m_0}\widetilde M$, is flat for the Riemannian metric induced by $\tilde g$.
elements.sent-2411-039: In \cite{BCH , we recently proved a rigidity result of a dynamical nature.}
elements.sent-2411-040: The main result proved in this paper is geometric and complements the study started while also opens a series of research questions.
elements.sent-2411-041: The main ingredient in the proof of Theorem \ref{main is the Riccati equation satisfied by the shape operator of the horospheres.}
elements.sent-2411-042: The use of Riccati equation in the study of global geometric properties of Riemannian manifolds is not new, cf.
elements.sent-2411-043: For example, it is central in L.. W.
elements.sent-2411-044: Green's proof that Riemannian manifolds without conjugate points have non positive total scalar curvature and zero total scalar curvature if and only if the metric is Euclidean.
elements.sent-2411-045: More recently, M.~Itoh and H.~Satoh characterized the real space forms, i.e., the Cartan Hadamard manifolds of constant non positive sectional curvature, by the property that the horospheres are totally umbilic, i.e., have constant principal curvatures, \cite{IS .}
elements.sent-2411-046: In this latter work, the Riccati equation also plays an essential role.
elements.sent-2411-047: In \cite{ISS , the authors also give various characterizations of the complex and quaternionic hyperbolic spaces.}
elements.sent-2411-048: It is important to note that \cite{Knieper , \cite{IS and \cite{ISS provide characterizations of hyperbolic real space in term of the {\sl extrinsic geometry of horospheres.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-049: We recall that given $\tilde m\in \widetilde M$ and $\tilde v\in T^1_{\tilde m}\widetilde M$ there is a unique horosphere $H(\tilde v)\subset \widetilde M$, orthogonal to $\tilde v$ at $\tilde m$.
elements.sent-2411-050: When $(M, g)$ is a closed negatively curved manifold the horospheres are smooth and carry the Riemannian metric induced by $\tilde g$.
elements.sent-2411-051: Our assumptions are concerned with its scalar curvature, denoted by $s(\tilde v)$, which is a continuous function defined on $T^1\widetilde M$.
elements.sent-2411-052: The natural projection $\pi: \widetilde M\rightarrow M$ maps $H(\tilde v)$ to an immersed hypersurface in $M$.
elements.sent-2411-053: By abuse of notation, it will be denoted by $H(v)$, where $m=\pi (\tilde m)$ and $v= d\pi (\tilde v)\in T^1_m(M)$.
elements.sent-2411-054: By invariance by the deck transformations, the scalar curvature descends to a continuous function defined on $T^1M$, and for $v\in T^1M$ we denote it by $s(v)$.
elements.sent-2411-055: We also denote by $\mu_L$ the Liouville measure on $T^1M$.
elements.sent-2411-056: We now turn to the proof of our Main Theorem.
elements.sent-2411-057: Assertion $ii)$ implies that the scalar curvature function, $s(\cdot)$, is non negative on the lift to $T^1(M)$ of one horosphere.
elements.sent-2411-058: We first note that since $(M, g)$ is {\sl negatively curved $s(\cdot)$ is then non negative everywhere in $T^1M$.. More precisely,.}
elements.sent-2411-059: Let $(M^n, g)$ be a closed Riemannian manifold of dimension $n\geq 3$ and of negative sectional curvature.
elements.sent-2411-060: If one horosphere has non negative scalar curvature then every horosphere has non negative scalar curvature.
elements.sent-2411-062: The proof of this proposition relies on the density of each horosphere viewed as a leaf of the unstable foliation in the unit tangent bundle $T^1M$.
elements.sent-2411-063: The proof follows exactly the same lines as the one given for the second assertion of Proposition 2.1 in \cite{BCH .}
elements.sent-2411-064: We now proceed to the proof of the main theorem.
elements.sent-2411-065: To $\tilde m \in H(\tilde v)$ for $\tilde v\in T_{\tilde m}\widetilde M$ we associate $S:=S_{\tilde v}$ the shape operator of $H(\tilde v)$ as follows: let $\tilde u\in T^1_{\tilde m H(\tilde v)$, then \begin{equation}S: \tilde u\to\nabla_{\tilde u}\tilde N\in T^1_{\tilde m}H(\tilde v) ,\end{equation} where $\tilde N$ is the unit normal vector field of $H(\tilde v)$ (pointing outward).}
elements.formula-2411-002: \begin{equation}S: \tilde u\to\nabla_{\tilde u}\tilde N\in T^1_{\tilde m}H(\tilde v) ,\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-066: Note that some authors choose another possible convention for the shape operator, this one is chosen so that it is positive on a standard Euclidean sphere.
elements.sent-2411-067: Since the metric on $\tilde M$ is negatively curved, the Busemann functions are convex, hence, $S$ is a non negative symmetric operator acting on the tangent space to this horosphere at $\tilde m$.
elements.sent-2411-068: The shape operator is a $(1,1)$-tensor (see \cite{Eschenburg ) and we can also view it as the second fundamental form of $H(\tilde v)$ at $\tilde v$, which is a $(2,0)$-tensor.}

elements.sent-2411-081: The second term can be integrated using Fubini's decomposition, the integral of the quadratic form $\Ric$ on the unit sphere at a point is equal to its trace, $\Scal$ (the scalar curvature of $M$) divided by $n$, the dimension of $M$.
